verses.text: David asks God to 'hear the right' and let his vindication come from God's own presence. He appeals to the righteous Judge. The upright can ask God to weigh their cause, confident He sees the truth.
David asks God to 'hear the right' and let his vindication come from God's own presence. He appeals to the righteous Judge. The upright can ask God to weigh their cause, confident He sees the truth.

God has tested his heart in the night and found him resolved that his mouth shall not transgress; he has kept from the paths of the destroyer. He welcomes the searching. A conscience void of offence can bear God's examination.

God has tested his heart in the night and found him resolved that his mouth shall not transgress; he has kept from the paths of the destroyer. He welcomes the searching. A conscience void of offence can bear God's examination.

God has tested his heart in the night and found him resolved that his mouth shall not transgress; he has kept from the paths of the destroyer. He welcomes the searching. A conscience void of offence can bear God's examination.

He calls on God to show His marvellous lovingkindness, to keep him 'as the apple of the eye' and hide him 'under the shadow of thy wings.' He pleads tender protection. God guards His own as the most precious and sheltered of treasures.

He calls on God to show His marvellous lovingkindness, to keep him 'as the apple of the eye' and hide him 'under the shadow of thy wings.' He pleads tender protection. God guards His own as the most precious and sheltered of treasures.

He calls on God to show His marvellous lovingkindness, to keep him 'as the apple of the eye' and hide him 'under the shadow of thy wings.' He pleads tender protection. God guards His own as the most precious and sheltered of treasures.

He calls on God to show His marvellous lovingkindness, to keep him 'as the apple of the eye' and hide him 'under the shadow of thy wings.' He pleads tender protection. God guards His own as the most precious and sheltered of treasures.
His enemies, fat with success and proud of speech, stalk him like a lion greedy for prey. He feels hunted by cruel foes. The malice of the wicked is real, but it cannot reach beyond what God permits.
His enemies, fat with success and proud of speech, stalk him like a lion greedy for prey. He feels hunted by cruel foes. The malice of the wicked is real, but it cannot reach beyond what God permits.
His enemies, fat with success and proud of speech, stalk him like a lion greedy for prey. He feels hunted by cruel foes. The malice of the wicked is real, but it cannot reach beyond what God permits.

David asks God to arise and deliver him, then closes, 'I will behold thy face in righteousness: I shall be satisfied, when I awake, with thy likeness.' His hope is not in this world's portion. To wake at last in God's likeness is the believer's full and final joy.
